condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type VI (Hers Disease)
description: Liver glycogen phosphorylase deficiency causing hepatomegaly, growth retardation, and fasting hypoglycemia. Generally milder than other hepatic GSDs.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type IXa (X-linked Liver Phosphorylase Kinase Deficiency)
description: Liver-specific phosphorylase kinase alpha subunit deficiency causing hepatomegaly, growth delay, and fasting ketosis. Most common phosphorylase kinase deficiency.
Phosphorylase kinase beta subunit deficiency affecting both liver and muscle. Causes hepatomegaly, hypoglycemia, and mild muscle weakness.
Liver phosphorylase kinase gamma subunit deficiency. More severe than IXa with risk of liver fibrosis and cirrhosis if untreated.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type IXd (Muscle Phosphorylase Kinase Deficiency)
description: Muscle-specific phosphorylase kinase deficiency causing exercise intolerance, muscle cramps, and myoglobinuria. Liver function is normal.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type 0a (Liver Glycogen Synthase Deficiency)
description: Liver glycogen synthase deficiency causing fasting hypoglycemia and postprandial hyperglycemia with ketosis. Unique GSD with decreased rather than increased glycogen.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type 0b (Muscle Glycogen Synthase Deficiency)
description: Muscle glycogen synthase deficiency causing exercise intolerance, muscle weakness, and cardiomyopathy. Extremely rare condition with few reported cases.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type V (McArdle Disease)
description: Muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency causing exercise intolerance, myalgia, and second wind phenomenon. Risk of rhabdomyolysis with intense exercise.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type VII (Tarui Disease)
description: Muscle phosphofructokinase deficiency causing exercise intolerance, compensated hemolysis, and hyperuricemia. Symptoms worsen with carbohydrate intake.

condition: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation Type Ib (MPI-CDG)
description: Mannose phosphate isomerase deficiency causing protein-losing enteropathy, hepatic fibrosis, and coagulation defects. Treatable with oral mannose supplementation.

condition: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation Type Ic (ALG6-CDG)
description: Glucosyltransferase deficiency causing hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures, and strabismus. Second most common CDG type after PMM2-CDG.

condition: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation Type Id (ALG3-CDG)
description: Mannosyltransferase deficiency causing severe developmental delay, microcephaly, optic atrophy, and iris coloboma. Poor prognosis with early death.

condition: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation Type Ig (ALG12-CDG)
description: Alpha-1,6-mannosyltransferase deficiency causing psychomotor retardation, hypotonia, facial dysmorphism, and immune deficiency with frequent infections.

condition: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation Type Ih (ALG8-CDG)
description: Glucosyltransferase 2 deficiency causing hepatomegaly, coagulation abnormalities, protein-losing enteropathy, and cataracts. High early mortality.
Peroxin 6 deficiency causing peroxisome biogenesis disorder with variable severity from neonatal-lethal to milder Heimler syndrome phenotype.
Peroxin 12 deficiency causing classic Zellweger syndrome with craniofacial dysmorphism, neuronal migration defects, hepatomegaly, and renal cysts.
Peroxin 10 deficiency causing cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, and retinitis pigmentosa in milder cases or severe neonatal presentation.
Peroxin 26 deficiency causing variable peroxisome biogenesis disorder from severe neonatal adrenoleukodystrophy to mild late-onset ataxia.
Straight-chain acyl-CoA oxidase deficiency causing neonatal hypotonia, seizures, and progressive white matter disease resembling neonatal adrenoleukodystrophy.
Peroxisomal enzyme deficiency causing adult-onset sensorimotor neuropathy, pigmentary retinopathy, and elevated pristanic acid. Variable severity and age of onset.
First enzyme of urea cycle deficiency causing neonatal hyperammonemia and encephalopathy. Treatable with N-carbamylglutamate (Carbaglu) supplementation.
Final urea cycle enzyme deficiency causing progressive spastic diplegia, intellectual disability, and episodic hyperammonemia. Unique among UCDs for prominent neurological features.
Mitochondrial ornithine transporter deficiency causing ornithine accumulation, episodic hyperammonemia, and progressive neurological deterioration with coagulopathy.
4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase deficiency causing elevated tyrosine with variable intellectual disability and intermittent ataxia. Mildest form of tyrosinemia.
Pterin-4-alpha-carbinolamine dehydratase deficiency causing transient hyperphenylalaninemia. Generally benign with spontaneous normalization of phenylalanine levels.
BH4 synthesis defect causing severe neurotransmitter deficiency. Most common cause of malignant hyperphenylalaninemia requiring dopamine and serotonin precursor therapy.
BH4 regeneration defect causing severe hyperphenylalaninemia with neurotransmitter deficiency. Progressive neurological deterioration despite dietary treatment.
BH4 synthesis defect causing childhood-onset progressive dystonia with marked diurnal fluctuation. Dramatic and sustained response to low-dose levodopa therapy.
E1-beta subunit deficiency of branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase causing classic MSUD with neonatal encephalopathy and maple syrup odor in urine.
E2 dihydrolipoyl transacylase deficiency causing branched-chain amino acid accumulation. Rare form with similar clinical presentation to classic MSUD.
Propionyl-CoA carboxylase beta subunit deficiency causing life-threatening ketoacidosis, hyperammonemia, and cardiomyopathy. Requires protein-restricted diet.
Adenosylcobalamin synthesis defect causing methylmalonic aciduria responsive to vitamin B12 supplementation. Generally milder than mut-type MMA.
ATP:cob(I)alamin adenosyltransferase deficiency causing methylmalonic aciduria with variable B12 responsiveness. May have better prognosis than mut-type.
Leucine catabolism defect causing 3-methylcrotonylglycinuria. Highly variable phenotype from asymptomatic to metabolic crisis with developmental delay.
3-Methylglutaconyl-CoA hydratase deficiency causing mild organic aciduria with speech delay. Generally favorable prognosis compared to other MGA types.
Mitochondrial trifunctional protein alpha subunit deficiency causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia, cardiomyopathy, and peripheral neuropathy. Risk of HELLP syndrome in pregnancy.
TFP beta subunit deficiency causing long-chain fatty acid oxidation defect with cardiomyopathy, hepatopathy, and myopathy. Severe neonatal form is often fatal.
VLCAD deficiency causing three phenotypes: severe neonatal cardiomyopathy, childhood hepatic/hypoketotic hypoglycemia, or adult-onset myopathy with rhabdomyolysis.
Electron transfer flavoprotein alpha deficiency causing multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency. Severe form causes neonatal metabolic crisis and congenital anomalies.
Electron transfer flavoprotein beta deficiency causing MADD. Riboflavin-responsive forms exist with milder late-onset myopathic presentation.
Lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficiency of alpha-L-iduronidase, resulting in accumulation of dermatan and heparan sulfate in tissues.
Late-onset form of GM2 gangliosidosis caused by partial deficiency of hexosaminidase A, presenting with progressive neurodegeneration.
Lysosomal storage disorder caused by N-acetylgalactosamine-6-sulfatase deficiency, causing skeletal dysplasia and corneal clouding.
Lysosomal storage disorder with progressive intellectual disability caused by alpha-N-acetylglucosaminidase deficiency.
Lysosomal storage disorder caused by arylsulfatase B deficiency, resulting in dermatan sulfate accumulation and skeletal abnormalities.
Progressive neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disorder presenting with vision loss, seizures, and cognitive decline in childhood.
Rare lysosomal storage disorder caused by beta-glucuronidase deficiency, with variable presentation from hydrops fetalis to mild skeletal disease.
Most common form of Sanfilippo syndrome, caused by heparan N-sulfatase deficiency leading to progressive neurodegeneration.
Rare lysosomal storage disorder caused by beta-mannosidase deficiency, presenting with intellectual disability and recurrent infections.
Rare lysosomal storage disorder caused by acid ceramidase deficiency, characterized by painful joint swelling, subcutaneous nodules, and hoarse voice.
Lysosomal storage disorder caused by defective cystinosin transporter, leading to cystine accumulation and progressive renal failure.
Neurovisceral lysosomal storage disorder caused by impaired intracellular cholesterol trafficking, leading to progressive neurological deterioration.
Inborn error of phenylalanine metabolism caused by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to intellectual disability if untreated.
Disorder of methionine metabolism caused by cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency, resulting in lens dislocation, thromboembolism, and skeletal abnormalities.
Disorder of tyrosine metabolism caused by tyrosine aminotransferase deficiency, presenting with corneal erosions and palmar keratoses.
Rare disorder of tyrosine catabolism caused by 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase deficiency, presenting with metabolic acidosis in infancy.

condition: Maple Syrup Urine Disease Type IA
description: Branched-chain amino acid metabolism disorder caused by BCKD E1-alpha deficiency, presenting with sweet-smelling urine and encephalopathy.

condition: Maple Syrup Urine Disease Type IB
description: Branched-chain amino acid catabolism defect caused by BCKD E1-beta subunit deficiency, presenting with neonatal metabolic crisis.
Inherited disorder of renal amino acid transport causing excessive cystine excretion and recurrent kidney stone formation.
Amino acid transport disorder caused by mutations in the light subunit of the cystine transporter, leading to recurrent nephrolithiasis.
Urea cycle-related disorder caused by arginase I deficiency, presenting with progressive spastic diplegia and intellectual disability.
Disorder of the urea cycle and citrin transporter, presenting with neonatal cholestasis or adult-onset hyperammonemia and neuropsychiatric symptoms.
Organic acidemia caused by methylmalonyl-CoA mutase deficiency, presenting with metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and developmental delay.
Organic acidemia caused by propionyl-CoA carboxylase alpha subunit deficiency, presenting with life-threatening metabolic decompensation in neonates.
Organic acidemia caused by propionyl-CoA carboxylase beta subunit deficiency, resulting in ketoacidosis and neurological complications.
Vitamin B12-responsive organic acidemia caused by defective mitochondrial cobalamin transport, with milder course than mut-type MMA.
Organic acidemia caused by defective adenosylcobalamin synthesis, presenting with metabolic acidosis and developmental delay.
Organic acidemia caused by acyl-CoA synthetase family member 3 deficiency, with variable clinical severity from asymptomatic to developmental delay.
Rare organic acidemia caused by D-2-hydroxyglutarate dehydrogenase deficiency, presenting with epilepsy, macrocephaly, and cardiomyopathy.
Most common fatty acid oxidation disorder, presenting with hypoketotic hypoglycemia during fasting or illness, preventable with frequent feeding.
Fatty acid oxidation defect causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia, cardiomyopathy, and hepatic dysfunction during metabolic stress.
Long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia, cardiomyopathy, and retinopathy.
Fatty acid oxidation disorder affecting hepatic CPT I, causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia and hepatomegaly during fasting.
Severe fatty acid oxidation disorder caused by defective mitochondrial carnitine shuttle, presenting with neonatal cardiac arrest.
Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency caused by electron transfer flavoprotein alpha defect, with variable severity from neonatal lethal to late-onset myopathy.
Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency caused by electron transfer flavoprotein beta defect, presenting with metabolic acidosis and hypoglycemia.
Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency caused by ETF-ubiquinone oxidoreductase defect, often responsive to riboflavin therapy.
Hepatic glycogen synthase deficiency causing fasting hypoglycemia with postprandial hyperglycemia and hyperlactatemia.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type Ia (von Gierke)
description: Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency causing severe fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis, and hyperlipidemia.
Glucose-6-phosphate translocase deficiency with symptoms similar to GSD Ia plus neutropenia and recurrent infections.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type III (Cori Disease)
description: Glycogen debranching enzyme deficiency causing hepatomegaly, hypoglycemia, and progressive myopathy and cardiomyopathy.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type IV (Andersen Disease)
description: Glycogen branching enzyme deficiency causing progressive hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis, usually fatal in early childhood.

condition: Glycogen Storage Disease Type IXa (Phosphorylase Kinase Deficiency)
description: X-linked hepatic phosphorylase kinase deficiency causing hepatomegaly and mild fasting hypoglycemia, usually resolving by adulthood.
Muscle phosphoglycerate mutase deficiency causing exercise intolerance and recurrent myoglobinuria.
Peroxisome biogenesis disorder with a spectrum from severe neonatal Zellweger syndrome to milder infantile Refsum disease phenotype.
Peroxisomal disorder caused by PEX7 receptor deficiency, characterized by rhizomelic limb shortening, cataracts, and intellectual disability.
Peroxisomal disorder of phytanic acid metabolism presenting with retinitis pigmentosa, peripheral neuropathy, and cerebellar ataxia.
Peroxisomal plasmalogen synthesis defect causing rhizomelic limb shortening and stippled calcifications in cartilage.
Peroxisome biogenesis disorder caused by PEX5 receptor deficiency, with impaired import of peroxisomal matrix proteins.
Mitochondrial respiratory chain disorder caused by SURF1 assembly factor deficiency, presenting with Leigh syndrome and lactic acidosis.
Fatal infantile cardioencephalomyopathy caused by cytochrome c oxidase assembly defect, presenting with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Most common respiratory chain defect presenting with leukoencephalopathy, cardiomyopathy, or Leigh syndrome.
Mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma deficiency causing Alpers syndrome with progressive liver failure and intractable seizures.
Mitochondrial nucleotide metabolism defect causing progressive liver failure and neurological deterioration in infancy.
Thymidine kinase 2 deficiency causing progressive skeletal myopathy with mitochondrial DNA depletion in muscle tissue.
Succinate-CoA ligase deficiency causing mtDNA depletion with encephalomyopathy, methylmalonic aciduria, and hearing loss.
Ribonucleotide reductase deficiency causing severe infantile mitochondrial DNA depletion with renal tubulopathy and seizures.
Urea cycle disorder caused by argininosuccinate synthetase deficiency, presenting with hyperammonemia and elevated citrulline.
Second most common urea cycle disorder caused by argininosuccinate lyase deficiency, presenting with hyperammonemia and liver disease.
Urea cycle disorder with distinct presentation of progressive spasticity rather than acute hyperammonemia seen in other UCD.
Amino acid transport defect causing impaired intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption of cationic amino acids with hyperammonemia.
Galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase deficiency causing toxic galactose metabolite accumulation with liver failure and sepsis in neonates.
Milder form of galactosemia caused by galactokinase deficiency, primarily presenting with bilateral cataracts.
Rare galactosemia variant ranging from benign peripheral form to severe generalized disease mimicking classic galactosemia.
Gluconeogenesis disorder presenting with episodes of fasting hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis, typically triggered by illness.
GLUT2 transporter deficiency causing hepatorenal glycogen accumulation, fasting hypoglycemia, and renal tubular dysfunction.
SGLT1 transporter deficiency causing severe osmotic diarrhea in neonates upon ingestion of glucose or galactose.
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young caused by glucokinase deficiency, resulting in mild stable fasting hyperglycemia rarely requiring treatment.
Anaplerotic enzyme deficiency causing lactic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and impaired gluconeogenesis with variable severity.
X-linked mitochondrial energy metabolism defect causing lactic acidosis, structural brain anomalies, and developmental delay.
Variant form of glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency with later onset and milder striatal involvement.
Rare porphyria caused by uroporphyrinogen III synthase deficiency, presenting with severe photosensitivity and hemolytic anemia.
Extremely rare hepatic porphyria caused by delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase deficiency, presenting with acute neurovisceral crises.
Most common CYP2D6 null allele in Europeans, causing poor metabolism of codeine, tamoxifen, and many antidepressants.
CYP2D6 frameshift deletion allele causing absent enzyme activity and poor metabolism of multiple drugs.
Common CYP2D6 variant associated with reduced enzyme activity and intermediate metabolism of substrates including tramadol and venlafaxine.
Most common reduced-function CYP2D6 allele in East Asians, causing decreased metabolism of codeine and antidepressants.
CYP2D6 reduced-function allele common in individuals of African descent, causing decreased metabolism of risperidone and codeine.
CYP2D6 reduced-function allele found primarily in Sub-Saharan African populations, affecting metabolism of multiple drug classes.
CYP2D6 frameshift allele causing complete loss of enzyme function and poor metabolizer status for substrates.
CYP2D6 decreased-function allele causing reduced splicing efficiency and intermediate metabolizer phenotype.
Common CYP2D6 haplotype marker found on multiple alleles, associated with variable enzyme activity depending on full haplotype.
CYP2D6 in-frame deletion allele with reduced enzyme activity, contributing to intermediate metabolizer phenotype.
Most common CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele, causing poor metabolism of clopidogrel with increased cardiovascular event risk.
CYP2C19 premature stop codon allele common in East Asians, causing absent enzyme activity for clopidogrel and proton pump inhibitors.
CYP2C19 gain-of-function allele causing increased enzyme activity, leading to reduced efficacy of proton pump inhibitors and voriconazole.
Rare CYP2C19 non-functional allele causing complete loss of clopidogrel activation when homozygous.
Rare CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele with clinical relevance for clopidogrel and escitalopram dosing.
CYP2C19 decreased-function allele that may affect clopidogrel response and antidepressant metabolism.
CYP2C19 non-functional allele caused by arginine to histidine substitution in the heme-binding region.
CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele with disrupted exon 1 start codon affecting translation initiation.
CYP2C19 splice site variant causing aberrant mRNA processing and absent enzyme protein expression.
Common CYP2C19 coding variant used as a haplotype marker, generally not associated with altered drug metabolism.
CYP2C9 reduced-function allele requiring lower warfarin doses, common in European populations.
CYP2C9 markedly reduced-function allele requiring significantly lower warfarin and phenytoin doses.
CYP2C9 loss-of-function allele found primarily in African-American populations, affecting warfarin and NSAID metabolism.
CYP2C9 frameshift variant with absent enzyme activity found in African-descent populations, relevant for warfarin dosing.
CYP2C9 reduced-function allele enriched in African-American populations with implications for warfarin sensitivity.
CYP2C9 reduced-function allele affecting the substrate recognition site with reduced clearance of warfarin and losartan.
CYP2C9 reduced-function variant with clinical significance for warfarin dosing and NSAID metabolism.
CYP2C9 decreased-function allele found in East and Southeast Asian populations, influencing warfarin dose requirements.
Most common CYP3A5 variant causing cryptic splice site and loss of functional protein, affecting tacrolimus dosing.
CYP3A5 splice variant found primarily in African populations causing absent enzyme expression and altered tacrolimus metabolism.
Rare CYP3A5 frameshift allele causing loss of functional enzyme protein, relevant for tacrolimus dose optimization.
CYP3A5 missense variant with reduced catalytic activity affecting immunosuppressant and calcium channel blocker metabolism.
CYP3A5 missense variant with decreased enzyme stability, contributing to variable tacrolimus pharmacokinetics.
DPYD splice site variant causing complete loss of dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase, contraindicated for 5-fluorouracil and capecitabine.
DPYD missense variant with no residual enzyme activity, associated with severe life-threatening fluoropyrimidine toxicity.
DPYD reduced-function variant requiring fluoropyrimidine dose reduction to prevent severe mucositis and myelosuppression.
Deep intronic DPYD variant causing partial exon skipping and reduced DPD activity, requiring fluoropyrimidine dose reduction.
Common DPYD missense variant used as a haplotype marker, with uncertain independent effect on fluoropyrimidine toxicity.
DPYD missense variant with modest reduction in enzyme activity, contributing to variable fluoropyrimidine toxicity risk.
Common DPYD coding variant with minor effect on enzyme activity, used in composite genotype scoring for fluoropyrimidine dosing.
DPYD variant in linkage with the deep intronic c.1129-5923C>G mutation, serving as a proxy marker for reduced DPD activity.

condition: TPMT Poor Metabolizer (*2 allele) - Thiopurine Toxicity
description: TPMT loss-of-function allele causing severe myelosuppression with standard doses of azathioprine and 6-mercaptopurine.

condition: TPMT Intermediate Metabolizer (*3B allele) - Thiopurine Sensitivity
description: TPMT reduced-function allele requiring thiopurine dose reduction to prevent life-threatening myelosuppression.

condition: TPMT Intermediate Metabolizer (*3C allele) - Thiopurine Sensitivity
description: Most common TPMT reduced-function allele globally, particularly in East Asian populations, affecting thiopurine metabolism.
Rare TPMT variant with decreased enzyme activity found in haplotype with *3A and *3B alleles.
Rare TPMT loss-of-function allele that completely abolishes enzyme activity when homozygous.
TPMT non-functional allele causing absent enzyme activity, contraindicated for standard thiopurine dosing.
UGT1A1 promoter TA repeat variant causing reduced bilirubin glucuronidation and increased risk of irinotecan-induced neutropenia.
UGT1A1 upstream variant in linkage with *28 allele, associated with reduced enzyme expression and irinotecan sensitivity.
UGT1A1 variant associated with modestly reduced enzyme activity and mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
UGT1A1 upstream regulatory variant affecting transcription, linked to Gilbert syndrome and irinotecan toxicity risk.
UGT1A1 variant in strong linkage with *28, used as a tag SNP for predicting irinotecan toxicity risk.
VKORC1 promoter variant causing reduced enzyme expression and requiring significantly lower warfarin doses for therapeutic anticoagulation.
VKORC1 intronic variant in linkage with -1639G>A, associated with lower warfarin dose requirements across populations.
VKORC1 3'-UTR variant associated with altered mRNA stability and variable warfarin dose requirements.
VKORC1 intronic variant used as haplotype marker for warfarin sensitivity grouping in pharmacogenomic-guided dosing.
Rare VKORC1 coding variant causing warfarin resistance, requiring extremely high doses for therapeutic anticoagulation.

condition: SLCO1B1 Reduced Function (*5 allele) - Statin Myopathy
description: SLCO1B1 reduced-function transporter variant increasing simvastatin plasma levels and risk of statin-induced myopathy.
SLCO1B1 variant associated with increased hepatic uptake transporter activity and potentially lower statin plasma concentrations.
SLCO1B1 haplotype marker associated with reduced transporter activity and increased risk of statin-related adverse effects.
SLCO1B1 coding variant that may modify the effect of *5 allele on statin transport when present on the same haplotype.
SLCO1B1 intronic variant in strong linkage with *5 allele, used in GWAS as marker for simvastatin myopathy risk.
SLCO1B1 missense variant with minor effect on transporter function, relevant for composite genotype-based statin prescribing.

condition: NAT2 Slow Acetylator (*5 allele) - Isoniazid Toxicity
description: Most common NAT2 slow-acetylator allele in Europeans, increasing risk of isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy.

condition: NAT2 Slow Acetylator (*6 allele) - Drug Sensitivity
description: Common NAT2 reduced-function allele affecting metabolism of isoniazid, hydralazine, and sulfonamides.

condition: NAT2 Slow Acetylator (*7 allele) - Drug Sensitivity
description: NAT2 reduced-function allele common in East Asians, causing slow acetylation of isoniazid and increased toxicity risk.
NAT2 coding variant used as a rapid-acetylator haplotype marker, associated with normal isoniazid metabolism.
NAT2 synonymous variant serving as haplotype marker for slow-acetylator status prediction in clinical pharmacogenomics.
Intergenic variant serving as robust tag SNP for NAT2 acetylator phenotype prediction in genome-wide association studies.
NAT2 synonymous variant in linkage with *5 allele, commonly included in clinical NAT2 genotyping panels.
Tag SNP for HLA-B*5701 allele, mandatory screening before abacavir initiation to prevent life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction.
Tag SNP for HLA-B*5801 allele associated with allopurinol-induced Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis.
Tag SNP for HLA-A*3101 allele associated with carbamazepine-induced maculopapular exanthema and DRESS syndrome.
Marker near HLA-B*1502 allele, required screening in Southeast Asian patients before carbamazepine to prevent Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
Tag SNP for HLA-DRB1*0701 allele associated with increased risk of drug-induced liver injury from lapatinib and ximelagatran.
HLA class I variant tagging the common HLA-A*0201 allele, modifying risk of drug-induced immune reactions to multiple medications.
HLA class II variant associated with altered immune response to drug-protein adducts, modifying hypersensitivity susceptibility.
Tag SNP associated with HLA-B*1301 allele and increased risk of dapsone hypersensitivity syndrome.
HLA class II variant associated with anti-HMGCR antibody-mediated autoimmune myopathy triggered by statin therapy.
HLA class II tag SNP associated with lapatinib-induced hepatotoxicity, used in pre-treatment risk stratification.
Common COMT functional variant affecting catecholamine metabolism, influencing pain sensitivity and opioid analgesic requirements.
TLR4 variant affecting innate immune signaling, modifying response to opioids and susceptibility to drug-induced inflammatory reactions.
ANKK1 variant near DRD2 gene affecting dopamine receptor density and response to antipsychotic medications.
DRD2 synonymous variant affecting mRNA stability and receptor expression, influencing antipsychotic drug efficacy and side effects.
MDR1/ABCB1 variant affecting P-glycoprotein expression and drug efflux, influencing bioavailability of digoxin, cyclosporine, and many drugs.
MDR1/ABCB1 non-synonymous variant affecting drug transport across blood-brain barrier, influencing CNS drug concentrations.
MDR1/ABCB1 synonymous variant in linkage with functional variants, used in haplotype-based P-glycoprotein phenotype prediction.

condition: ACE Insertion/Deletion Tag - ACE Inhibitor Response
description: ACE gene variant tagging the insertion/deletion polymorphism, influencing ACE inhibitor efficacy for hypertension and heart failure.

condition: AGTR1 A1166C - Angiotensin Receptor Blocker Response
description: Angiotensin II receptor type 1 variant affecting receptor expression and blood pressure response to ARB therapy.
Hepatic organic anion transporter variant affecting methotrexate clearance and risk of delayed elimination toxicity.
MTHFR variant reducing enzyme activity, associated with increased methotrexate toxicity due to impaired folate metabolism.
Common MTHFR thermolabile variant reducing enzyme activity by 70% when homozygous, affecting methotrexate and folate-pathway drug response.
MTHFR variant with modest effect on enzyme activity, compounding methotrexate toxicity risk when combined with C677T.
Glutathione S-transferase variant affecting detoxification of platinum chemotherapy drugs, influencing treatment efficacy and ototoxicity risk.
MRP2 transporter variant affecting methotrexate biliary excretion and risk of delayed drug elimination.
MRP2 promoter variant reducing transporter expression and affecting disposition of multiple drugs including methotrexate and irinotecan.
BCRP transporter variant reducing drug efflux, increasing rosuvastatin exposure and affecting allopurinol-lowering efficacy for gout.
Reduced folate carrier variant affecting methotrexate cellular uptake, influencing treatment response in rheumatoid arthritis and cancer.

condition: APOE e2 Allele - Statin Response Modifier
description: APOE variant defining the e2 allele, associated with enhanced LDL-cholesterol lowering response to statin therapy.

condition: APOE e4 Allele - Drug Response Modifier
description: APOE variant defining the e4 allele, associated with reduced statin efficacy and altered Alzheimer disease drug responses.

condition: BRCA1-Related Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer
description: Pathogenic BRCA1 variant significantly increases lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer. Carriers have up to 72% risk of breast cancer by age 80.
Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation in BRCA1. One of three founder mutations with high penetrance for breast and ovarian cancer.
Common Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation in BRCA1 associated with early-onset breast cancer and high ovarian cancer risk.
Pathogenic BRCA2 variant conferring elevated risk of breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. Lifetime breast cancer risk up to 69%.
Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation in BRCA2. Increases risk for breast, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers.
Pathogenic missense variant in BRCA2 DNA repair domain. Associated with increased risk of breast and ovarian malignancies.

condition: Lynch Syndrome Type I - MLH1 Deficiency
description: MLH1 mismatch repair gene variant causing Lynch syndrome. Carriers face 52-82% lifetime risk of colorectal cancer and elevated endometrial cancer risk.

condition: Lynch Syndrome - MLH1 Splice Site Variant
description: Splice-site variant in MLH1 causing exon skipping and loss of mismatch repair function. Associated with early-onset colorectal cancer.

condition: Lynch Syndrome Type II - MSH2 Deficiency
description: Pathogenic MSH2 variant causing Lynch syndrome with high risk of colorectal, endometrial, ovarian, and urothelial cancers.
Frameshift mutation in MSH2 resulting in truncated protein and defective DNA mismatch repair. Highly penetrant for colorectal cancer.

condition: Lynch Syndrome Type V - MSH6 Deficiency
description: MSH6 mismatch repair variant associated with later-onset Lynch syndrome. Particularly elevated risk for endometrial cancer in women.

condition: Lynch Syndrome Type IV - PMS2 Deficiency
description: PMS2 variant causing Lynch syndrome with moderate penetrance. Lifetime colorectal cancer risk of 15-20%, lower than MLH1/MSH2 variants.
TP53 germline mutation causing Li-Fraumeni syndrome with dramatically elevated risk of sarcomas, breast cancer, brain tumors, and adrenocortical carcinoma.
Recurrent TP53 hotspot mutation at codon 248. Associated with particularly aggressive cancer phenotype in Li-Fraumeni families.
Common TP53 gain-of-function mutation disrupting DNA binding domain. Carriers develop multiple primary cancers often before age 30.
RB1 tumor suppressor gene mutation causing hereditary retinoblastoma. Bilateral eye tumors typically present in early childhood.
Nonsense mutation in RB1 causing premature stop codon. Associated with bilateral retinoblastoma and increased risk of secondary osteosarcoma.
APC gene mutation causing hundreds to thousands of colorectal adenomas. Without prophylactic colectomy, colorectal cancer develops by age 40.
APC variant in the attenuated region causing fewer polyps with later onset colorectal cancer risk compared to classic FAP.
Common APC I1307K variant in Ashkenazi Jewish population. Modestly increases colorectal cancer risk by approximately twofold.
VHL tumor suppressor mutation causing renal cell carcinoma, hemangioblastomas of the CNS and retina, and pheochromocytomas.
VHL missense variant associated with high pheochromocytoma risk but lower renal cell carcinoma risk than classic VHL syndrome.
MEN1 gene mutation causing tumors of the parathyroid glands, pituitary, and pancreatic islets. Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common manifestation.
MEN1 variant with high penetrance for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Gastrinomas are the most common functional pancreatic tumors in MEN1.
RET proto-oncogene mutation causing medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and parathyroid hyperplasia in MEN2A.
RET M918T mutation causing the most aggressive MEN2 subtype with early-onset medullary thyroid cancer, mucosal neuromas, and marfanoid habitus.
PTEN loss-of-function variant causing Cowden syndrome spectrum. Elevated risk for breast, thyroid, endometrial, and renal cancers.
PTEN missense variant causing Cowden syndrome characterized by mucocutaneous lesions, macrocephaly, and elevated cancer risk across multiple organs.
CDH1 E-cadherin mutation causing hereditary diffuse gastric cancer. Lifetime gastric cancer risk of 70-80% with lobular breast cancer risk in women.
CDH1 variant associated with hereditary lobular breast cancer. Female carriers have up to 42% lifetime risk of invasive lobular breast cancer.
CHEK2 I157T variant conferring moderate breast cancer risk. Also associated with increased risk of colorectal and prostate cancers.
CHEK2 1100delC truncating variant doubling breast cancer risk. Most prevalent in Northern European populations.
PALB2 partner and localizer of BRCA2 variant. Confers 3-5 fold increased breast cancer risk and elevated pancreatic cancer susceptibility.
Truncating PALB2 variant associated with 33-58% lifetime breast cancer risk and significantly elevated pancreatic cancer risk.
Heterozygous ATM variant conferring moderate breast cancer risk. Homozygous loss causes ataxia-telangiectasia.
ATM missense variant associated with increased risk of breast and pancreatic cancers. Moderate penetrance with 2-3 fold risk increase.
BAP1 germline mutation predisposing to uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, renal cell carcinoma, and cutaneous melanocytic tumors.
BAP1 loss-of-function variant significantly increasing mesothelioma susceptibility independent of asbestos exposure.
MUTYH Y179C founder mutation. Biallelic carriers develop multiple colorectal adenomas and have high colorectal cancer risk.
MUTYH G396D variant, the second most common MUTYH mutation. Biallelic carriers develop adenomatous polyposis with elevated colorectal cancer risk.
STK11 serine/threonine kinase mutation causing hamartomatous GI polyps, mucocutaneous pigmentation, and markedly elevated cancer risk.
Truncating STK11 variant causing Peutz-Jeghers syndrome. Cumulative cancer risk reaches 85% by age 70.
NF1 neurofibromin gene mutation causing cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, and optic gliomas. Increased risk of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors.
NF1 missense variant causing classic neurofibromatosis features. Variable expressivity ranges from mild cutaneous findings to malignant transformation.
NF2 merlin gene mutation causing bilateral vestibular schwannomas, meningiomas, and spinal tumors. Hearing loss typically presents in teenage years.
NF2 variant associated with schwannomatosis phenotype. Multiple schwannomas without vestibular involvement and chronic pain are hallmarks.
TSC1 hamartin gene mutation causing cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, cardiac rhabdomyomas, and renal angiomyolipomas.
TSC2 tuberin gene mutation causing tuberous sclerosis. TSC2 mutations generally cause more severe disease than TSC1.
TSC2 variant with prominent pulmonary lymphangioleiomyomatosis presentation. Primarily affects women causing progressive cystic lung disease.

condition: Familial Atypical Multiple Mole Melanoma Syndrome
description: CDKN2A p16 tumor suppressor mutation causing hereditary melanoma syndrome. Lifetime melanoma risk of 60-90% with elevated pancreatic cancer risk.
CDKN2A variant associated with familial melanoma and pancreatic cancer. Pancreatic cancer risk increased 13-22 fold in carrier families.
WT1 transcription factor mutation predisposing to childhood Wilms tumor. May also cause Denys-Drash syndrome with nephropathy.
WT1 deletion causing WAGR syndrome with Wilms tumor, aniridia, genitourinary anomalies, and intellectual disability.
ALK receptor tyrosine kinase germline mutation predisposing to familial neuroblastoma. Accounts for most hereditary neuroblastoma cases.
PHOX2B transcription factor variant associated with neuroblastoma and congenital central hypoventilation syndrome.
SMAD4 mutation causing juvenile polyposis with hamartomatous GI polyps and increased colorectal cancer risk.
BMPR1A bone morphogenetic protein receptor variant causing juvenile polyposis. Colorectal cancer risk reaches 40-50% without surveillance.
SDHA succinate dehydrogenase subunit A variant predisposing to paragangliomas and pheochromocytomas.
SDHB variant with highest malignancy risk among SDH gene mutations. Associated with extra-adrenal paragangliomas and renal cell carcinoma.
SDHC variant causing hereditary paragangliomas primarily in the head and neck region. Lower malignancy risk than SDHB-associated tumors.
SDHD variant causing paragangliomas primarily through paternal inheritance. Most commonly presents with head and neck paragangliomas.
FLCN folliculin gene mutation causing fibrofolliculomas, pulmonary cysts with spontaneous pneumothorax, and chromophobe renal cell carcinoma.
FLCN truncating variant with prominent renal cancer risk. Approximately 25-35% of affected individuals develop renal tumors.
SMARCB1 chromatin remodeling gene mutation predisposing to malignant rhabdoid tumors in infancy and schwannomatosis in adults.
DICER1 RNase III mutation predisposing to pleuropulmonary blastoma, cystic nephroma, and ovarian Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors.
DICER1 variant associated with multinodular goiter and differentiated thyroid carcinoma.
SUFU suppressor of fused gene variant predisposing to desmoplastic medulloblastoma in infancy and basal cell carcinomas.
PTCH1 hedgehog pathway mutation causing Gorlin syndrome with multiple basal cell carcinomas, odontogenic keratocysts, and skeletal anomalies.
PTCH1 variant with elevated childhood medulloblastoma risk. About 5% of Gorlin patients develop desmoplastic medulloblastoma.
BRIP1 BRCA1-interacting protein helicase variant conferring moderate ovarian cancer risk. Lifetime ovarian cancer risk approximately 5-10%.
RAD51C homologous recombination repair gene variant. Moderate risk for ovarian cancer and possible breast cancer susceptibility.
RAD51D DNA repair gene variant associated with moderate ovarian cancer risk. PARP inhibitor sensitivity may apply.
EPCAM deletion causing epigenetic silencing of MSH2 and Lynch syndrome. Primarily elevated colorectal and endometrial cancer risk.
HOXB13 G84E variant significantly increasing prostate cancer risk, particularly early-onset disease.
Heterozygous NBN nibrin variant conferring moderate breast cancer risk. Homozygous mutations cause Nijmegen breakage syndrome.
CHEK2 variant associated with moderate colorectal cancer risk in addition to breast cancer susceptibility.
BRCA1 variant with strong association to triple-negative breast cancer subtype. PARP inhibitor therapy may be effective in carriers.
BRCA2 variant with elevated pancreatic cancer risk. Family history of pancreatic cancer increases penetrance significantly.

condition: Lynch Syndrome - MLH1 Promoter Methylation Risk
description: Constitutional MLH1 epimutation predisposing to Lynch syndrome cancers through cis-acting regulatory variants.
MSH2 variant associated with Muir-Torre syndrome phenotype of Lynch syndrome, featuring sebaceous skin tumors and visceral malignancies.
TP53 R337H Brazilian founder mutation with high penetrance for childhood adrenocortical carcinoma.
APC variant in the Gardner syndrome region causing FAP with prominent extracolonic manifestations including desmoid tumors and osteomas.
VHL missense variant associated with both pheochromocytoma and clear cell renal carcinoma. Requires aggressive renal surveillance.
PTEN variant causing Bannayan-Riley-Ruvalcaba syndrome with macrocephaly, lipomas, hemangiomas, and genital lentigines.
MEN1 variant with predominant pituitary adenoma phenotype. Prolactinomas are the most common pituitary tumor in MEN1 patients.
RET variant causing familial medullary thyroid carcinoma without other MEN2 features. Prophylactic thyroidectomy recommended.
CDH1 variant associated with hereditary diffuse gastric cancer and increased risk of orofacial clefting in carrier families.
Biallelic PALB2 mutations cause Fanconi anemia subtype N with childhood solid tumors. Heterozygous carriers have moderate breast cancer risk.
ATM heterozygous carrier variant. Carriers have 2-3 fold increased breast cancer risk while homozygous loss causes ataxia-telangiectasia.
SDHA variant predisposing to SDH-deficient gastrointestinal stromal tumors and paragangliomas.
SDHB variant with elevated renal cell carcinoma risk in addition to paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma susceptibility.

condition: Hereditary Leiomyomatosis and Renal Cell Cancer
description: Fumarate hydratase mutation causing cutaneous and uterine leiomyomas with aggressive type 2 papillary renal cell carcinoma.
FH variant with high penetrance for collecting duct renal cell carcinoma. Even small renal masses require prompt surgical intervention.
MET receptor tyrosine kinase activating mutation causing bilateral, multifocal type 1 papillary renal cell carcinoma.
MITF E318K variant conferring 2-fold increased melanoma risk and 5-fold increased renal cell carcinoma risk.
MSH6 mismatch repair variant with particularly high endometrial cancer penetrance in women carriers.
Biallelic PMS2 mutations cause constitutional mismatch repair deficiency with childhood-onset brain tumors and hematologic malignancies.
Biallelic NTHL1 base excision repair mutations causing adenomatous polyposis and elevated colorectal cancer risk.
POT1 shelterin complex variant predisposing to familial melanoma and glioma. Affects telomere protection and chromosomal stability.
Beta-myosin heavy chain mutation causing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy. Leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes.
MYH7 R403Q missense variant associated with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. High penetrance with early-onset disease and increased sudden death risk.
MYH7 converter domain mutation causing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with variable expressivity. Moderate to severe phenotype depending on additional modifiers.
Cardiac myosin-binding protein C truncating variant. Most common genetic cause of HCM with later onset but progressive hypertrophy.

condition: MYBPC3 Founder Mutation - South Asian HCM
description: MYBPC3 25bp deletion founder mutation in South Asian populations. Causes late-onset HCM and heart failure with 4-6% carrier frequency in India.
Cardiac troponin T mutation causing HCM with mild hypertrophy but high arrhythmia risk. Disproportionate sudden death risk relative to wall thickness.
TNNT2 variant causing dilated cardiomyopathy with progressive systolic dysfunction. May present in childhood or young adulthood.
Cardiac troponin I mutation causing HCM with apical hypertrophy phenotype. Associated with restrictive physiology in some families.
TNNI3 variant causing primary restrictive cardiomyopathy with diastolic dysfunction. Often presents in childhood with poor prognosis.
Lamin A/C mutation causing dilated cardiomyopathy with conduction disease. High risk of sudden cardiac death requiring early ICD consideration.
LMNA variant causing Emery-Dreifuss phenotype with skeletal muscle involvement and dilated cardiomyopathy. Conduction defects precede cardiomyopathy.
Titin truncating variant, the most common genetic cause of dilated cardiomyopathy. A-band truncations carry highest risk for heart failure.
TTN truncating variant associated with peripartum cardiomyopathy. Women carriers have elevated risk of heart failure during or after pregnancy.
SCN5A sodium channel loss-of-function variant causing Brugada syndrome with characteristic ECG pattern and risk of ventricular fibrillation.
SCN5A variant causing dilated cardiomyopathy with arrhythmia. Distinct from Brugada syndrome with progressive ventricular dysfunction.
SCN5A gain-of-function variant causing LQT3 with prolonged QT interval at rest. Events often occur during sleep with high lethality per event.
Desmoplakin truncating variant causing arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy with left ventricular involvement. High risk of ventricular arrhythmias.
DSP variant causing left-dominant arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy with episodic myocarditis-like presentations and fibrofatty replacement.
Plakophilin-2 mutation, the most common cause of ARVC. Progressive fibrofatty replacement of the right ventricle with ventricular arrhythmias.
PKP2 frameshift variant causing classic ARVC with epsilon waves on ECG and exercise-triggered ventricular tachycardia.
Desmoglein-2 desmosomal variant causing arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. Biventricular involvement more common than with PKP2 mutations.
DSG2 variant presenting as dilated cardiomyopathy rather than classic ARVC. Progressive heart failure with arrhythmia risk.
Biallelic KCNQ1 mutation causing Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome with severe QT prolongation and congenital sensorineural deafness.
KCNH2 pore region mutation associated with longer QTc and higher cardiac event rate. Pore mutations carry worse prognosis than non-pore variants.
RYR2 variant in the central or C-terminal domain hotspot. Highly arrhythmogenic with calcium leak from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
LDL receptor mutation causing familial hypercholesterolemia with severely elevated LDL cholesterol and premature coronary artery disease.
LDLR null allele causing severe familial hypercholesterolemia. Homozygous carriers develop coronary disease in childhood if untreated.
APOB R3527Q variant causing familial defective apolipoprotein B-100. Impaired LDL receptor binding leads to elevated LDL cholesterol.
PCSK9 gain-of-function variant causing increased LDL receptor degradation and severe hypercholesterolemia. Target of PCSK9 inhibitor therapy.
PCSK9 loss-of-function variant conferring lifelong low LDL cholesterol levels and reduced coronary heart disease risk. Protective variant.
FBN1 variant in exons 24-32 associated with severe neonatal Marfan syndrome. Early onset with rapidly progressive aortic and mitral valve disease.
FBN1 missense variant with high risk of aortic dissection. Requires aggressive blood pressure control and serial aortic imaging.
TGFBR1 mutation causing Loeys-Dietz syndrome with aggressive aortopathy, arterial tortuosity, and aneurysms throughout the arterial tree.
TGFBR2 mutation causing Loeys-Dietz syndrome. Aortic dissection occurs at smaller diameters than Marfan syndrome, requiring earlier surgical intervention.
TGFBR2 kinase domain mutation with particularly aggressive vascular phenotype. Widespread arterial aneurysms beyond the aorta.
ACVRL1 variant with prominent hepatic arteriovenous malformations. May cause high-output heart failure and portal hypertension.
BMPR2 bone morphogenetic protein receptor mutation causing heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension. Presents earlier and more severely than idiopathic PAH.
BMPR2 truncating variant with higher penetrance for pulmonary arterial hypertension. Non-missense variants associated with earlier disease onset.
Cardiac actin gene mutation causing HCM with apical hypertrophy. Also associated with congenital septal defects in some families.
Alpha-tropomyosin mutation causing HCM with variable penetrance. Generally associated with milder phenotype than MYH7 or MYBPC3 variants.
Myosin regulatory light chain variant causing midventricular HCM. Distinct phenotype with midcavity obstruction rather than typical LVOT obstruction.
Essential myosin light chain variant causing HCM with midventricular hypertrophy. Rare cause of HCM predominantly reported in specific populations.
Phospholamban R14del Dutch founder mutation causing DCM with high arrhythmia burden. Low-voltage ECG and biventricular involvement are characteristic.
PLN variant causing cardiomyopathy with features overlapping both DCM and ARVC. Progressive fibrosis with malignant ventricular arrhythmias.
Filamin C truncating variant causing DCM with high arrhythmogenic potential. Myocardial fibrosis is a prominent feature with sudden death risk.
Desmin intermediate filament mutation causing myofibrillar myopathy with restrictive or dilated cardiomyopathy and conduction defects.
DES variant causing restrictive cardiomyopathy with skeletal myopathy. Characteristic desmin-positive aggregates in cardiac and skeletal muscle.
SMAD3 mutation causing aneurysms-osteoarthritis syndrome with aortic aneurysms, early-onset osteoarthritis, and mild craniofacial features.
TGFB2 ligand mutation causing familial thoracic aortic aneurysm with Marfan-like features. Variable expressivity with mitral valve prolapse.
TGFB3 variant causing the mildest Loeys-Dietz subtype with later onset aortopathy and fewer extra-aortic features.
KCNJ2 inward rectifier potassium channel mutation causing long QT with periodic paralysis and characteristic facial dysmorphism.
CACNA1C calcium channel gain-of-function variant causing severe QT prolongation, syndactyly, autism, and extremely high sudden death risk.
KCNE1 minK potassium channel accessory subunit variant causing LQT5. Mild QT prolongation with variable penetrance.
KCNE2 MiRP1 variant causing LQT6 with drug-induced QT prolongation susceptibility. Most events triggered by medications rather than exercise.
Calmodulin CALM1 variant causing CPVT or long QT syndrome. Ultra-rare with severe phenotype often presenting in infancy.
Calsequestrin-2 mutation causing autosomal recessive CPVT. Exercise-induced bidirectional VT with structurally normal heart.
MYBPC3 variant causing dilated rather than hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in some families. Demonstrates phenotypic overlap between HCM and DCM.
MYH7 variant causing left ventricular noncompaction cardiomyopathy with prominent trabeculations and thin compacted layer.
Tafazzin gene mutation causing Barth syndrome with dilated cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, skeletal myopathy, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria in males.
LAMP2 lysosomal membrane protein mutation causing Danon disease with severe HCM, Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, and intellectual disability in males.
Alpha-galactosidase A variant causing cardiac Fabry disease with progressive LV hypertrophy. Late-onset form may mimic sarcomeric HCM.
MYBPC3 Icelandic founder variant causing late-onset HCM with heart failure. High prevalence in Iceland contributing to heart failure burden.
LMNA variant presenting with isolated atrial fibrillation before development of overt cardiomyopathy. Early AF is a red flag for LMNA disease.
SCN5A loss-of-function variant causing familial sick sinus syndrome with bradycardia, sinus arrest, and atrial standstill.
HCN4 funny channel mutation causing inappropriate sinus bradycardia and chronotropic incompetence. Some variants also associated with noncompaction.
KCNQ1 gain-of-function variant causing short QT syndrome with abbreviated QTc interval and increased risk of atrial and ventricular fibrillation.
KCNH2 gain-of-function variant causing SQT1 with very short QTc and tall peaked T waves. High risk of sudden cardiac death.
Smooth muscle alpha-actin mutation causing familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection. Some variants associated with early-onset stroke and Moyamoya.
Smooth muscle myosin heavy chain mutation causing thoracic aortic aneurysm with patent ductus arteriosus. Ascending aortic aneurysm predominates.
NOTCH1 variant associated with familial bicuspid aortic valve and early calcific aortic stenosis. Aortopathy may co-occur.
ABCC6 variant causing pseudoxanthoma elasticum with progressive arterial calcification, peripheral vascular disease, and coronary artery disease.
Fibrillin-2 mutation causing Beals syndrome with arachnodactyly, joint contractures, and mild aortic root dilation. Generally less severe than Marfan.
Type III collagen mutation causing vascular EDS with arterial rupture, bowel perforation, and uterine rupture. Median survival 51 years.
COL3A1 glycine substitution in the triple helical domain causing severe vascular EDS. Glycine substitutions generally more severe than haploinsufficiency.
AMP-activated protein kinase gamma-2 mutation causing cardiac glycogen storage with ventricular pre-excitation and progressive conduction disease.
TMEM43 S358L Newfoundland founder mutation causing fully penetrant ARVC with high sudden death rate. Males affected more severely than females.
Desmocollin-2 desmosomal variant causing ARVC. Biventricular involvement common with both right and left ventricular fibrofatty replacement.
Plakoglobin mutation causing Naxos disease triad of ARVC, palmoplantar keratoderma, and woolly hair. Fully penetrant by adolescence.
Biallelic MYBPC3 variants causing severe neonatal cardiomyopathy with heart failure in infancy. Dramatically more severe than heterozygous HCM.
RNA-binding motif protein 20 variant causing aggressive DCM with early onset and high arrhythmia burden. Titin mis-splicing is the key mechanism.
APC variant associated with Turcot syndrome presenting with colorectal polyposis and medulloblastoma. Brain tumors occur in approximately 1% of FAP families.
BAG3 co-chaperone truncating variant causing DCM with myofibrillar myopathy features. Progressive heart failure typically in the fourth decade.
Cardiac ankyrin repeat domain protein variant causing DCM through disrupted sarcomere signaling. Rare cause of familial DCM.
KCNJ5 potassium channel variant causing aldosterone-producing adenomas and severe hypertension with hypokalemia. Important cause of secondary hypertension.
KCNA5 voltage-gated potassium channel loss-of-function variant causing early-onset lone atrial fibrillation without structural heart disease.
Connexin 40 gap junction variant causing familial atrial fibrillation through impaired atrial conduction and electrical coupling.
Apolipoprotein A-V variant causing severe hypertriglyceridemia and recurrent pancreatitis. Triglyceride levels may exceed 1000 mg/dL.
cGMP-dependent protein kinase 1 gain-of-function variant causing thoracic aortic aneurysm and acute aortic dissection at young ages.

condition: Bicuspid Aortic Valve with Aortopathy - SMAD6
description: SMAD6 variant associated with bicuspid aortic valve, thoracic aortic aneurysm, and congenital heart defects including craniosynostosis.
OCTN2 carnitine transporter deficiency causing dilated cardiomyopathy, skeletal myopathy, and hypoketotic hypoglycemia. Treatable with carnitine supplementation.
Alpha-dystrobrevin variant causing left ventricular noncompaction with congenital heart defects. Prominent trabeculations with risk of heart failure and thromboembolism.
TRPM4 cation channel gain-of-function variant causing progressive cardiac conduction disease with right bundle branch block and complete heart block.
Emerin gene mutation causing X-linked Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy with cardiomyopathy, conduction defects, and atrial paralysis. Sudden death risk requires ICD.
Alpha-B crystallin mutation causing myofibrillar myopathy with hypertrophic or dilated cardiomyopathy and posterior subcapsular cataracts.
Most common autosomal dominant ataxia worldwide, presenting with progressive cerebellar ataxia and pyramidal signs.
X-linked demyelinating neuropathy caused by connexin-32 mutations, with more severe presentation in males.
Axonal form of CMT caused by mitofusin-2 mutations affecting mitochondrial fusion and axonal transport.
Demyelinating peripheral neuropathy caused by myelin protein zero mutations with variable onset and severity.
Severe infantile-onset motor neuron disease caused by SMN1 deletions, leading to progressive muscular weakness and respiratory failure.
Intermediate form of SMA with onset between 6-18 months; patients achieve sitting but not independent ambulation.
Milder form of SMA with onset after 18 months; patients achieve independent walking but may lose ambulation over time.
Severe X-linked muscular dystrophy caused by dystrophin deficiency, with progressive proximal weakness and cardiomyopathy.
Milder X-linked dystrophinopathy with later onset and slower progression compared to Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
Most common limb-girdle dystrophy caused by calpain-3 deficiency, with selective proximal limb and trunk weakness.
Dysferlinopathy causing proximal weakness and markedly elevated creatine kinase due to defective membrane repair.
Alpha-sarcoglycanopathy presenting with Duchenne-like progressive proximal weakness, often with calf hypertrophy.
Dystroglycanopathy with variable severity ranging from mild limb-girdle weakness to severe congenital muscular dystrophy.
Progressive motor neuron degeneration caused by toxic gain-of-function mutations in copper-zinc superoxide dismutase.
Aggressive form of familial ALS caused by FUS mutations leading to abnormal RNA processing and cytoplasmic protein aggregation.
Motor neuron disease caused by TDP-43 mutations with cytoplasmic mislocalization and aggregation of the RNA-binding protein.
Most common genetic cause of both ALS and FTD, caused by hexanucleotide repeat expansion in C9orf72.
Most common form of hereditary spastic paraplegia caused by spastin mutations, with progressive lower limb spasticity.
Spastic paraplegia with cerebellar ataxia caused by paraplegin deficiency affecting mitochondrial quality control.
Complex spastic paraplegia with thin corpus callosum, cognitive impairment, and peripheral neuropathy due to spatacsin deficiency.
Early-onset hereditary spastic paraplegia caused by atlastin-1 mutations affecting ER morphology and axonal transport.
Severe epileptic encephalopathy beginning in infancy with febrile seizures progressing to refractory epilepsy and cognitive decline.
Early-onset epileptic encephalopathy with variable seizure types caused by gain-of-function mutations in the Nav1.2 sodium channel.
Neonatal-onset epileptic encephalopathy caused by potassium channel mutations, ranging from benign neonatal seizures to severe encephalopathy.
Episodic ataxia with myokymia caused by potassium channel mutations, triggered by stress and sudden movement.
Severe brain malformation with smooth cerebral surface caused by LIS1 mutations disrupting neuronal migration during development.
Lissencephaly in males and subcortical band heterotopia in females caused by doublecortin mutations affecting neuronal migration.
Neurodevelopmental disorder primarily affecting girls, with regression of language and hand skills, stereotypic hand movements, and seizures.
Severe congenital muscular dystrophy with white matter abnormalities caused by laminin alpha-2 chain deficiency.
Severe congenital muscular dystrophy with joint hyperlaxity and proximal contractures caused by collagen VI deficiency.
Mild collagen VI-related myopathy with proximal weakness and prominent finger flexion contractures.
Severe infantile neurovisceral storage disease with hepatosplenomegaly and rapid neurodegeneration due to acid sphingomyelinase deficiency.
Adult-onset neurodegenerative variant with progressive motor neuron disease, cerebellar ataxia, and psychiatric symptoms.
Mitochondrial disorder with progressive external ophthalmoplegia, epilepsy, and hepatopathy caused by DNA polymerase gamma mutations.
Progressive mitochondrial encephalopathy with bilateral basal ganglia lesions caused by cytochrome c oxidase assembly defect.
Mitochondrial respiratory chain disorder presenting with leukoencephalopathy, cardiomyopathy, or Leigh syndrome.
Purine metabolism disorder with intellectual disability, seizures, and autistic features due to succinyladenosine accumulation.
Peroxisomal disorder with cerebral demyelination and adrenal insufficiency caused by very-long-chain fatty acid accumulation.
Neurocutaneous disorder with congenital ichthyosis, spastic diplegia, and intellectual disability due to fatty aldehyde dehydrogenase deficiency.
X-linked purine metabolism disorder with hyperuricemia, dystonia, and compulsive self-injurious behavior due to HPRT deficiency.
X-linked copper transport disorder with progressive neurodegeneration, kinky hair, and connective tissue abnormalities.
X-linked epileptic encephalopathy with infantile spasms and intellectual disability caused by aristaless-related homeobox mutations.
Early-onset seizure disorder with severe intellectual disability and Rett-like features predominantly affecting females.
Severe neurodevelopmental disorder with microcephaly, dyskinesia, and absent language resembling congenital Rett syndrome.
Epileptic encephalopathy with involuntary movements caused by gain-of-function mutations in the inhibitory G-protein alpha subunit.
Early infantile epileptic encephalopathy caused by Munc18-1 haploinsufficiency disrupting synaptic vesicle release.
Epilepsy with myoclonic-atonic seizures and intellectual disability caused by GAT-1 GABA transporter haploinsufficiency.
Epilepsy spectrum including Landau-Kleffner syndrome with acquired aphasia and centrotemporal spikes due to NMDA receptor dysfunction.
Severe early-onset epileptic encephalopathy with sudden unexpected death risk caused by Nav1.6 sodium channel gain-of-function mutations.
Seizure disorder affecting females in clusters triggered by fever, with variable intellectual disability due to cellular interference mechanism.
Brain energy failure syndrome with seizures, movement disorder, and acquired microcephaly due to impaired cerebral glucose transport.
GABA degradation disorder with intellectual disability, ataxia, and seizures due to gamma-hydroxybutyric acid accumulation.
Organic acidemia with macrocephaly and acute striatal necrosis during metabolic crisis due to glutaryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency.
Branched-chain amino acid metabolic disorder causing neonatal encephalopathy with characteristic sweet odor and risk of brain edema.
Lysosomal storage disorder with neuroaxonal dystrophy and progressive psychomotor regression due to alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase deficiency.
GM2 gangliosidosis with progressive neurodegeneration, cherry-red macular spot, and macrocephaly due to hexosaminidase A and B deficiency.
Neurodegenerative MPS with severe behavioral disturbance and progressive cognitive decline due to alpha-N-acetylglucosaminidase deficiency.
Most common Sanfilippo syndrome subtype with progressive dementia, behavioral problems, and sleep disturbance due to heparan sulfatase deficiency.
Neuronopathic form of MPS I with progressive cognitive decline, hydrocephalus, and severe skeletal dysplasia due to alpha-L-iduronidase deficiency.

condition: Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay
description: Progressive spastic ataxia with peripheral neuropathy and retinal fiber hypermyelination caused by sacsin deficiency.
Progressive neurodegeneration with chorea, orofacial dyskinesia, and acanthocytosis caused by chorein deficiency.
Infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy with progressive motor and cognitive regression due to phospholipase A2 deficiency.
X-linked dominant neurodegeneration with iron accumulation, progressive dystonia-parkinsonism, and dementia in adulthood.
Childhood-onset dystonia with diurnal fluctuation and dramatic response to low-dose levodopa caused by GTP cyclohydrolase I deficiency.
Dopamine synthesis defect with infantile parkinsonism-dystonia responsive to levodopa therapy.
Movement disorder with myoclonic jerks and dystonia, often alcohol-responsive, caused by epsilon-sarcoglycan mutations with maternal imprinting.
Adolescent-onset primary dystonia predominantly affecting cranial and cervical muscles caused by THAP1 transcription factor mutations.
Non-progressive chorea with childhood onset caused by NKX2-1 mutations, often associated with thyroid and pulmonary abnormalities.
Most common inherited dystonia with childhood-onset limb dystonia that may generalize, caused by torsinA loss-of-function with reduced penetrance.
Most common form of SCID caused by gamma chain deficiency, resulting in absent T and NK cells with nonfunctional B cells.
SCID caused by toxic deoxyadenosine accumulation affecting lymphocyte development, one of the first diseases treated by gene therapy.
T-cell negative, B-cell positive SCID caused by IL-7 receptor deficiency disrupting thymic T-cell development.
SCID with increased radiosensitivity caused by Artemis nuclease deficiency impairing V(D)J recombination and DNA repair.
T-cell immunodeficiency caused by absent CD3 delta chain preventing proper T-cell receptor complex assembly.
Primary immunodeficiency with defective phagocyte respiratory burst leading to recurrent bacterial and fungal infections.
Primary B-cell immunodeficiency with absent immunoglobulins and recurrent sinopulmonary infections caused by Bruton tyrosine kinase deficiency.
CVID caused by inducible costimulator deficiency leading to impaired germinal center formation and hypogammaglobulinemia.
CVID associated with TACI mutations affecting B-cell maturation and class-switch recombination with variable penetrance.
Humoral immunodeficiency with reduced mature B cells caused by BAFF receptor deficiency impairing B-cell survival signaling.
Immunodeficiency with elevated IgM and absent IgG/IgA caused by CD40 ligand deficiency preventing immunoglobulin class switching.
Autosomal recessive hyper-IgM syndrome with lymphoid hyperplasia caused by AID deficiency preventing class-switch recombination.
Hyper-IgM immunodeficiency with defective class-switch recombination caused by uracil-DNA glycosylase mutations.
Classical complement pathway deficiency strongly associated with systemic lupus erythematosus and recurrent bacterial infections.
Most common complement deficiency in Caucasians, predisposing to encapsulated bacterial infections and SLE-like autoimmunity.
Severe complement deficiency with recurrent pyogenic infections and immune complex disease due to central complement component absence.
Terminal complement deficiency with susceptibility to recurrent Neisseria infections due to absent membrane attack complex formation.
Terminal complement deficiency with increased susceptibility to meningococcal disease and other Neisseria infections.
Terminal complement pathway deficiency predisposing to recurrent meningococcal meningitis and septicemia.
Terminal complement deficiency causing impaired MAC formation and increased risk of Neisseria meningitidis infection.
Most common complement deficiency in Japanese populations; usually clinically silent but may increase meningococcal disease risk.
Episodic subcutaneous and submucosal edema due to C1 inhibitor deficiency with potentially fatal laryngeal involvement.
Angioedema caused by dysfunctional C1 inhibitor protein with normal quantitative levels and similar clinical presentation to type I.
Hyperinflammatory syndrome with uncontrolled macrophage activation caused by perforin deficiency preventing cytotoxic lymphocyte function.
HLH caused by Munc13-4 deficiency impairing cytotoxic granule exocytosis with life-threatening hyperinflammation.
Rare HLH variant caused by syntaxin-11 deficiency affecting SNARE-mediated cytotoxic granule fusion.
HLH caused by Munc18-2 deficiency with additional colitis and hypogammaglobulinemia distinguishing it from other HLH subtypes.
Defective lymphocyte apoptosis causing chronic lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, autoimmune cytopenias, and elevated double-negative T cells.
Rare ALPS variant caused by Fas ligand deficiency with lymphoproliferation and lupus-like autoimmune manifestations.
ALPS caused by caspase-10 mutations disrupting death receptor-mediated apoptosis with lymphoproliferation and autoimmunity.
Immunodeficiency with markedly elevated IgE, recurrent staphylococcal abscesses, eczema, and skeletal abnormalities due to STAT3 dysfunction.
HIES with severe viral skin infections, food allergies, and T-cell lymphopenia caused by DOCK8 deficiency affecting immune cell migration.
Immunodeficiency with thymic hypoplasia, cardiac defects, and hypoparathyroidism caused by TBX1 haploinsufficiency in 22q11.2 deletion.
Severe T-cell immunodeficiency with congenital alopecia and nail dystrophy caused by thymic epithelial cell transcription factor deficiency.
Most common severe bleeding disorder caused by factor VIII deficiency with spontaneous hemarthroses and soft tissue hemorrhages.
Mild form of factor VIII deficiency with bleeding primarily after trauma or surgery, with residual factor activity 5-40%.
Bleeding disorder caused by factor IX deficiency, clinically indistinguishable from hemophilia A but five times less common.
Mild factor IX deficiency with bleeding after significant trauma and residual clotting activity above 5%.
Qualitative VWF defect with loss of high-molecular-weight multimers causing disproportionate mucocutaneous bleeding.
Gain-of-function VWF mutation with enhanced platelet binding causing thrombocytopenia and loss of large multimers.
Most thrombogenic inherited thrombophilia with 50% lifetime VTE risk due to reduced thrombin and factor Xa inhibition.
Most common inherited hemolytic anemia in Northern Europeans with spherical red cells, jaundice, and splenomegaly due to ankyrin deficiency.
Spherocytosis caused by band 3 protein mutations disrupting red cell membrane-cytoskeleton interactions.
Red cell membrane disorder common in malaria-endemic regions with oval stomatocytes, usually asymptomatic but protective against malaria.
Red cell membrane disorder with elliptical erythrocytes, usually mild hemolysis, caused by alpha-spectrin mutations.
Spherocytosis caused by beta-spectrin deficiency with variable severity from compensated hemolysis to transfusion dependence.
Spherocytosis caused by protein 4.2 deficiency affecting band 3-ankyrin interactions, more common in Japanese population.
Severe G6PD variant common in Mediterranean populations with very low residual enzyme activity and chronic hemolytic anemia.
Inherited pure red cell aplasia presenting in infancy with macrocytic anemia and physical anomalies caused by ribosomal protein deficiency.
Ribosomopathy with red cell aplasia and higher rate of cleft palate caused by RPL5 mutations affecting ribosome biogenesis.
DBA caused by large ribosomal subunit protein mutations with macrocytic anemia and thumb anomalies.
Most common Fanconi anemia subtype with progressive bone marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and cancer predisposition due to DNA repair defects.
Severe Fanconi anemia with high cancer risk caused by biallelic BRCA2 mutations; heterozygous carriers have breast/ovarian cancer predisposition.
Fanconi anemia subtype with bone marrow failure and chromosomal breakage caused by FANCG mutations in the FA core complex.
Telomere biology disorder with nail dystrophy, oral leukoplakia, skin hyperpigmentation, and bone marrow failure caused by dyskerin deficiency.
Telomeropathy with progressive bone marrow failure, pulmonary fibrosis, and liver disease caused by telomerase reverse transcriptase mutations.
Telomere maintenance disorder with mucocutaneous triad and bone marrow failure caused by telomerase RNA component mutations.
Severe dyskeratosis congenita with Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson features caused by RTEL1 helicase deficiency affecting telomere integrity.
Severe dyskeratosis congenita often with aplastic anemia in childhood caused by TIN2 shelterin complex mutations.
Severe neutropenia with absolute neutrophil count below 200/uL and life-threatening bacterial infections caused by elastase mutations.
Severe congenital neutropenia with accelerated apoptosis of myeloid progenitors caused by HCLS1-associated protein X-1 deficiency.
Neutropenia with cardiac and urogenital malformations caused by glucose-6-phosphatase catalytic subunit 3 deficiency.
Skeletal dysplasia with metaphyseal chondrodysplasia, fine sparse hair, and combined immunodeficiency caused by RMRP RNA mutations.

condition: MHC Class I Deficiency (Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type I)
description: Immunodeficiency with absent MHC class I surface expression causing recurrent respiratory infections and necrotizing granulomatous skin lesions.
Bare lymphocyte syndrome type I caused by TAP2 mutations preventing peptide transport for MHC class I antigen presentation.

condition: MHC Class II Deficiency (Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type II)
description: Severe combined immunodeficiency with absent MHC class II expression causing defective CD4+ T-cell selection and humoral immune failure.
Bare lymphocyte syndrome type II caused by CIITA master transactivator deficiency preventing MHC class II gene transcription.
Immunodeficiency with defective store-operated calcium entry causing impaired T-cell activation, autoimmunity, and ectodermal dysplasia.
Combined immunodeficiency with impaired calcium signaling causing recurrent infections, autoimmunity, and nonprogressive myopathy.
T-cell immunodeficiency with fatal EBV-driven lymphoproliferation caused by IL-2-inducible T-cell kinase deficiency.
Fatal susceptibility to EBV with fulminant infectious mononucleosis, lymphoma, and dysgammaglobulinemia caused by SAP deficiency.
X-linked immunodeficiency with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis and inflammatory bowel disease caused by XIAP deficiency.
Autoimmune syndrome with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and adrenal insufficiency caused by AIRE deficiency.
X-linked fatal autoimmune enteropathy with diabetes and eczema caused by regulatory T-cell deficiency due to FOXP3 mutations.
Immunodeficiency with autoimmunity, lymphoproliferation, and inflammatory bowel disease caused by LRBA deficiency affecting CTLA-4 recycling.
Immune dysregulation with lymphocytic organ infiltration and hypogammaglobulinemia caused by CTLA-4 checkpoint haploinsufficiency.
Primary immunodeficiency with lymphoproliferation, recurrent infections, and herpesvirus susceptibility from PI3K delta gain-of-function.
X-linked immunodeficiency with ectodermal dysplasia, susceptibility to mycobacteria, and impaired NF-kB signaling.
Innate immunity defect with susceptibility to invasive pyogenic bacterial infections in childhood due to impaired TLR signaling.
Innate immune deficiency clinically identical to IRAK-4 deficiency with life-threatening pyogenic infections improving with age.
Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease with disseminated BCG and nontuberculous mycobacterial infections.
MSMD with susceptibility to intracellular pathogens due to impaired IL-12-dependent IFN-gamma production.
Most common genetic etiology of MSMD with susceptibility to Salmonella and mycobacterial infections due to IL-12/23 receptor deficiency.
Immunodeficiency with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, autoimmunity, and susceptibility to intracellular pathogens caused by STAT1 gain-of-function mutations.
Classical EDS caused by COL5A1 mutations leading to defective type V collagen. Characterized by skin hyperextensibility, atrophic scarring, and joint hypermobility.
Classical EDS type 2 due to COL5A2 mutations affecting type V collagen alpha-2 chain. Presents with fragile skin, easy bruising, and generalized joint hypermobility.
Vascular EDS caused by COL3A1 mutations disrupting type III collagen. Life-threatening due to arterial, intestinal, and uterine rupture risk.
Hypermobile EDS associated with tenascin-X deficiency. Features chronic joint pain, recurrent dislocations, and soft velvety skin.
Dermatosparaxis EDS from ADAMTS2 deficiency affecting procollagen processing. Characterized by extreme skin fragility, sagging, and bruising.
Arthrochalasia EDS type 1 from COL1A1 exon 6 skipping. Causes severe joint hypermobility with congenital bilateral hip dislocation.
Arthrochalasia EDS type 2 from COL1A2 exon 6 skipping. Features congenital hip dislocation and severe generalized joint laxity.
Kyphoscoliotic EDS caused by lysyl hydroxylase deficiency. Presents with severe muscle hypotonia at birth, progressive scoliosis, and ocular fragility.
FKBP14-related EDS featuring kyphoscoliosis, myopathy, and sensorineural hearing loss. Caused by mutations in the FKBP14 peptidyl-prolyl isomerase gene.
Severe neonatal Marfan syndrome with early-onset aortic and mitral valve insufficiency. Typically fatal in infancy without aggressive surgical intervention.
Aneurysms-osteoarthritis syndrome from SMAD3 mutations. Features early-onset osteoarthritis combined with aortic aneurysms and dissections.
LDS type 4 from TGFB2 mutations causing thoracic aortic aneurysms. Milder phenotype with variable penetrance for craniofacial and skeletal features.
Mild OI from quantitative COL1A1 deficiency. Features frequent fractures with minimal trauma, blue sclerae, and early-onset hearing loss.
Lethal perinatal OI caused by structural COL1A1 defects. Results in extreme bone fragility with multiple intrauterine fractures and respiratory failure.
Progressively deforming OI from COL1A2 mutations. Causes severe short stature, progressive skeletal deformity, and dentinogenesis imperfecta.
Moderate OI with normal sclerae caused by COL1A2 mutations. Features moderate bone fragility, short stature, and variable dentinogenesis imperfecta.
OI type V caused by IFITM5 mutation with unique hyperplastic callus formation. Characterized by calcification of interosseous membranes and radial head dislocation.
OI type VI from SERPINF1 deficiency causing defective bone mineralization. Presents with moderate to severe fractures and distinctive fish-scale pattern on bone biopsy.
Recessive OI from CRTAP mutations disrupting collagen prolyl 3-hydroxylation. Severe to lethal phenotype with rhizomelia and white sclerae.
Severe OI from P3H1/LEPRE1 deficiency affecting collagen modification. Features severe growth deficiency, extreme skeletal undermineralization, and bulbous metaphyses.
OI type IX caused by cyclophilin B deficiency from PPIB mutations. Moderate to lethal severity with short stature, white sclerae, and progressive limb deformity.
Most common Stickler syndrome type from COL2A1 mutations. Features vitreoretinal degeneration with high risk of retinal detachment, hearing loss, and arthropathy.
Stickler syndrome type II from COL11A1 mutations with beaded vitreous. Features progressive sensorineural hearing loss and milder skeletal manifestations.
Non-ocular Stickler syndrome from COL11A2 mutations. Presents with hearing loss and skeletal abnormalities but without vitreoretinal degeneration.
